front: Fanfare: A series of six fanfares (1986–2016) dedicated to conductor Marin Alsop; responds to Copland's Fanfare for the Common Man with the same instrumentation
back: Fanfares for the Uncommon Woman (Joan Tower)

front: Fanfare: Honors 'risk-takers and adventurers'; first dedicated to Marin Alsop of the Baltimore Symphony
back: Fanfares for the Uncommon Woman (Joan Tower)

front: Fanfare: Its title alludes to Copland's piece that opens with timpani, bass drum, and tam-tam, then trumpets playing F, B-flat, F
back: Fanfares for the Uncommon Woman (Joan Tower)

front: Conductor: Dedicatee of Joan Tower's Fanfares for the Uncommon Woman; first woman to direct the Last Night of the Proms (2013); created the Taki Concordia Fellowship for female conductors
Orchestral Work: 1981 piece that opens with rapid percussion accompanying a G pedal point that slowly 'branches out'; Tower's first orchestral work; described as Beethoven-inspired
Composition: Chamber piece for the Da Capo Chamber Players featuring repeating sixteenth notes from Stravinsky's 'Shrovetide Fair'; inspired by figure skating
Composer: Won three Grammys for this 2008 orchestral piece
Composer: 'Mineral works' by this composer include Black Topaz and Silver Ladders

Composition: 'Mineral work' for piano and six instruments; inspired by the composer's own drawing of colored rays from a piano; features two percussionists each playing vibraphone, tom-toms, marimba, and temple block
Composer: Co-founded the Da Capo Chamber Players; American contemporary composer known for responding to Copland
Composer: This composer's In Memory (string quartet) was originally intended to memorialize friend Margaret Shafer but was redirected to victims of 9/11
Piano Quintet: Joan Tower's only piano quintet, named for a D.C. estate; opens with sostenuto D, E, F, then E-flat, F-sharp, E-natural, F
Concerto: One-movement flute concerto by Joan Tower dedicated to Juilliard professor Carol Wincenc
